title: Matcha roll cake
subtitle: Light matcha sponge rolled around whipped cream
excerpt: A thin matcha sponge rolled while still warm, then filled with lightly sweetened whipped cream once cool. The sponge bakes in under twelve minutes; the technique of rolling it hot sets the shape before it can crack. Vivid green against white cream.
sourceNotes: The Swiss roll format has been part of British and European baking since the mid-nineteenth century. The matcha variation draws on Japanese roll cake (ro-ru ke-ki) traditions that became widespread in Japan from the late twentieth century, using ceremonial or culinary grade matcha powder in the sponge. This recipe synthesises the standard European Swiss roll technique with the Japanese matcha cake tradition.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The rolled (unfilled) sponge can be made several hours ahead and kept wrapped in the baking paper at room temperature. Fill and slice within 2 hours of serving.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven; the thin sponge bakes in 10 to 12 minutes at high heat so it stays moist enough to roll

glossaryTerms.definition: Rolling the sponge while it is still hot (straight from the oven) trains the gluten and egg proteins into a curved shape before they set. If the sponge cools flat first, it will crack when you try to roll it. Turn it out onto a sheet of baking paper dusted with icing sugar, peel off the lining paper, then roll from the short end with the new paper inside. Leave to cool completely in this rolled shape before unrolling to fill.

content.text: Matcha sponge is denser in flavour than a plain Swiss roll but just as light in texture. The
is the only technical challenge. Everything else is a simple whisked sponge.

Heat the oven to 190 degrees C fan. Line a 33x23 cm baking tray with baking paper. Whisk 4 eggs and 100 g caster sugar together with a hand mixer on high speed for 4 to 5 minutes until the mixture is very pale, thick, and holds a ribbon when the beaters are lifted. Sift together 100 g plain flour and 2 tbsp matcha powder and fold gently into the egg mixture in three additions, using a large metal spoon. Work quickly to keep the air in. Pour into the prepared tin and spread to an even layer. Bake for 10 to 12 minutes until the sponge is just set and springs back when pressed lightly.

While the sponge bakes, dust a sheet of baking paper with 2 tbsp icing sugar. Turn the hot sponge out immediately onto the dusted paper. Peel off the lining paper carefully. Working from the short end nearest you, roll the sponge up tightly inside the new paper. Transfer to a cooling rack and leave to cool completely in the rolled position, about 30 minutes.

Whip 250 ml cold double cream with 2 tbsp icing sugar and 1 tsp vanilla extract to soft peaks. Carefully unroll the cooled sponge and spread the cream evenly over it, leaving a 2 cm clear border at the far end. Re-roll tightly, using the paper to help. Wrap in the paper and refrigerate for 15 minutes to firm before slicing. Dust with a little extra matcha or icing sugar before serving.

items.solution: The sponge was not rolled while still hot, or it overbaked and dried out. Roll immediately from the oven. The sponge should feel soft and pliable. If it baked too long, it loses the moisture needed to bend without cracking.

items.problem: The cream squeezes out at the ends when sliced
items.solution: The cream was whipped too stiff, or too much was used at the ends. Spread the cream to within 2 cm of the far edge only. Soft-peak cream spreads more evenly and is easier to control than stiffly whipped cream.
